theorem_title: Burnside theorem for transitive permutation groups of prime degree
theorem_statement: Let \(p\) be a prime, and let \(G\) be a transitive permutation group acting on a set \(\Omega\) with \(|\Omega|=p\). Then either \(G\) is doubly transitive on \(\Omega\), or \(G\) is solvable.
raw_proof_text:
Since \(G\) acts transitively on a set of size \(p\), the orbit-stabilizer theorem implies that \(p\mid |G|\). By Cauchy’s theorem, \(G\) contains an element of order \(p\). Such an element acts as a \(p\)-cycle on \(\Omega\). We identify \(\Omega\) with the finite field \(\mathbb F_p\) so that this \(p\)-cycle is the translation

\[
\tau(i)=i+1.
\]

Assume \(G\) is not doubly transitive. We will prove that \(G\) is solvable.

Consider the action of \(G\) on ordered pairs of distinct elements

\[
\Omega^{(2)}={(i,j)\in \mathbb F_p^2:i\ne j}.
\]

If \(G\) were transitive on \(\Omega^{(2)}\), then \(G\) would be doubly transitive. Since \(G\) is not doubly transitive, \(\Omega^{(2)}\) has at least two \(G\)-orbits.

The translation \(\tau\) preserves the difference \(i-j\), and for each fixed nonzero \(d\in\mathbb F_p^\times\), it acts transitively on the set of pairs

\[
{(i,j):i-j=d}.
\]

Therefore every \(G\)-orbit on ordered pairs is a union of whole difference classes. Choose a nonempty proper \(G\)-orbit \(\mathcal O\subsetneq \Omega^{(2)}\), and define

\[
U={i-j:(i,j)\in \mathcal O}\subseteq \mathbb F_p^\times.
\]

Then \(U\) is nonempty and proper. Moreover, for every \(\pi\in G\), if \(i-j\in U\), then \((i,j)\in\mathcal O\), hence

\[
(\pi(i),\pi(j))\in \mathcal O,
\]

so

\[
\pi(i)-\pi(j)\in U.
\]

Thus every \(\pi\in G\) preserves the difference relation determined by \(U\).

We now use the following finite-field lemma.

**Finite-field affine lemma.** Let \(U\) be a nonempty proper subset of \(\mathbb F_p^\times\). Let \(\pi:\mathbb F_p\to\mathbb F_p\) be a permutation such that

\[
i-j\in U \implies \pi(i)-\pi(j)\in U
\]

for all \(i,j\in\mathbb F_p\). Then there exist \(a\in\mathbb F_p^\times\) and \(b\in\mathbb F_p\) such that

\[
\pi(i)=ai+b
\]

for every \(i\in\mathbb F_p\).

We give the proof of the lemma because it is the main local algebraic core.

First, since \(\pi\) is a finite permutation, some power \(\pi^m\) is the identity. The assumed implication applied repeatedly shows that

\[
i-j\in U
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\pi(i)-\pi(j)\in U.
\]

Replacing \(U\) by its complement in \(\mathbb F_p^\times\) if necessary, we may assume

\[
|U|\le \frac{p-1}{2}.
\]

For any fixed \(i\in\mathbb F_p\), the map

\[
u\mapsto \pi(i+u)-\pi(i)
\]

sends \(U\) into \(U\). It is injective because \(\pi\) is injective. Hence it is a bijection \(U\to U\). Therefore, for every integer \(w\ge 1\),

\[
\sum_{u\in U} \pi(i+u)^w
=

\sum_{u\in U}(\pi(i)+u)^w.
\]

Let (f(X)\in\mathbb F_p[X]) be the unique polynomial of degree at most \(p-1\) such that

\[
f(i)=\pi(i)
\]

for every \(i\in\mathbb F_p\). Let \(n=\deg f\). Since \(\pi\) is a permutation, \(n\ge 1\).

Suppose \(nw\le p-1\). Then the polynomial

\[
\sum_{u\in U} f(X+u)^w-\sum_{u\in U}(f(X)+u)^w
\]

has degree at most \(p-1\), and it vanishes at every element of \(\mathbb F_p\). Hence it is the zero polynomial.

Define the power sums

\[
S(k)=\sum_{u\in U}u^k.
\]

Choose the smallest \(r\ge 1\) such that \(S(r)\ne 0\). Such an \(r\) exists because \(S(p-1)=|U|\ne 0\) in \(\mathbb F_p\).

Expanding the identity above by the binomial theorem gives

\[
\sum_{u\in U}\bigl(f(X+u)^w-f(X)^w\bigr)
=

\sum_{k\ge 1}\binom{w}{k}S(k)f(X)^{w-k}.
\]

The right-hand side has leading contribution at \(k=r\), because (S(1)=\cdots=S(r-1)=0) and \(S(r)\ne 0\).

On the left-hand side, the leading term of \(f(X)^w\) has degree \(nw\). Translating \(X\mapsto X+u\) and subtracting cancels the top term, and the first possible nonzero leading contribution is controlled by the \(r\)-th power sum \(S(r)\). Comparing degrees in the polynomial identity shows the following: if \(r\le nw\), then necessarily

\[
nw-r\le n(w-r),
\]

which implies \(n=1\). Thus, unless \(f\) is linear, we must have

\[
r>nw
\]

for every \(w\) satisfying \(nw\le p-1\).

Now choose \(w\) maximal with \(nw\le p-1\). If \(n>1\), then

\[
p-1<n(w+1)\le 2nw.
\]

Since \(r>nw\), this gives

\[
r>\frac{p-1}{2}.
\]

Thus

\[
S(1)=S(2)=\cdots=S\left(\frac{p-1}{2}\right)=0.
\]

But \(|U|\le (p-1)/2\). If the first \(|U|\) power sums of a nonempty set \(U\subset \mathbb F_p^\times\) vanish, then the Vandermonde matrix on the elements of \(U\) would force all elements of \(U\) to be zero, a contradiction. More concretely, the vectors

\[
(1,u,u^2,\ldots,u^{|U|-1})
\]

for \(u\in U\) form a nonsingular Vandermonde matrix, so the vanishing of enough power sums is impossible for a nonempty set of nonzero field elements.

Therefore \(n=1\). Hence

\[
f(X)=aX+b
\]

with \(a\ne 0\), because \(f\) represents a permutation. Thus

\[
\pi(i)=ai+b
\]

for every \(i\in\mathbb F_p\). This proves the finite-field affine lemma.

Returning to the group \(G\), the lemma applies to every \(\pi\in G\). Hence every element of \(G\) is an affine map

\[
i\mapsto ai+b,
\qquad a\in\mathbb F_p^\times,\ b\in\mathbb F_p.
\]

Therefore

\[
G\le \operatorname{AGL}_1(\mathbb F_p)
=

\mathbb F_p\rtimes \mathbb F_p^\times.
\]

The additive group \(\mathbb F_p\) is abelian, and the multiplicative group \(\mathbb F_p^\times\) is abelian. Hence the affine group \(\mathbb F_p\rtimes \mathbb F_p^\times\) is solvable. Every subgroup of a solvable group is solvable, so \(G\) is solvable.

Thus a transitive group of prime degree is either doubly transitive or solvable.
